why_correct: Embedding a secret in a client-side bundle is not obfuscation — it's public disclosure. Any bearer token shipped to the browser is trivially extractable via DevTools, source inspection, or network sniffing, regardless of minification or bundling. The correct pattern is to keep the API key exclusively server-side, expose a first-party proxy endpoint to the frontend, and enforce authn/authz at that boundary. The trust boundary must sit between the client and your backend, never between your backend and Anthropic.

distractors.A: Spending caps are a billing guardrail, not a security control — this is the near-miss trap. A cap would have limited the financial damage but does nothing to prevent key extraction or unauthorized use. A would be the right answer if the question were 'how do you limit blast radius after a key is already exposed,' but the question asks what caused the failure, which is key exposure itself.
distractors.C: Anthropic API keys are not domain-scoped bearer tokens — there is no built-in CORS or origin restriction mechanism on the key itself. This misconception imports a mental model from OAuth or browser-restricted API keys (like Google Maps JS API keys) where domain allowlists are a real feature. Applying that pattern here produces false confidence: the key is still fully valid from any origin, including a server-controlled by the attacker.
distractors.D: HTTPS was almost certainly in use — modern browsers enforce it for most contexts, and the key would have been exposed in the bundle before any network request was made. Interception-in-transit is a different attack vector than static asset extraction.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic: secrets have exactly one valid location — server-side environment variables or secrets managers. The moment a credential crosses the trust boundary into client-reachable code, treat it as compromised by default. If the architecture requires the browser to call a third-party API directly, the answer is always a server-side proxy with your own authn layer, not creative key management.
cross_domain_note: This trust boundary principle resurfaces in agentic architectures — tool call results and injected context from external sources must also be treated as untrusted, for the same reason: anything outside your controlled execution environment is a potential attack surface.

why_correct: The root cause of formatting variance is underspecified output constraints in the prompt — Claude's default formatting behavior is context-sensitive and will adapt to perceived input signals (length, complexity, tone of the ticket). The fix is to encode the output contract explicitly in the system prompt: format type, length ceiling, required structure, and rendering context. This is the canonical pattern for deterministic output in production pipelines — you own the output schema, and the system prompt is where that schema lives.

distractors.A: Streaming controls delivery mechanism, not output structure. The rendering layer truncating a stream at 200 words doesn't prevent structural variance — you'll get inconsistently formatted partial responses, which is worse than long ones. Stream=True is the right lever when latency SLA requires first-token delivery optimization, not when you need format conformance.
distractors.B: max_tokens is a hard token budget, not a formatting instruction. A low max_tokens value will physically truncate responses, but Claude will still vary structure — you'll get arbitrarily cut-off bullet lists or sentences, not consistently formatted plain-text summaries. In production this produces malformed email bodies. The misconception is conflating length control with structural control.
distractors.D: Passing a prior assistant turn to anchor format is a brittle shot at few-shot prompting via conversation history, not explicit format specification. It breaks on cold starts, drifts over session boundaries, and gives Claude no grounding in why that format is required. This is the implicit-contract anti-pattern — it looks right until ticket content is sufficiently different and Claude deprioritizes the implied format signal.

mental_model: Whenever production output must conform to a fixed rendering contract, the system prompt is the enforcement point — not API parameters. The CCA heuristic: API parameters control compute behavior (tokens, sampling, streaming); system prompt controls output behavior (format, structure, persona, constraints). Never use a compute lever to solve a specification problem.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to the trust boundary domain — the system prompt is also where you constrain Claude's output surface for injection resistance. A tightly specified output schema (format, sections, length) reduces the jailbreak surface by limiting the degrees of freedom in the response space.

why_correct: The root cause is output format ambiguity — the model has no instruction constraining response structure, so it defaults to a conversational, explanatory style that's appropriate for human readers. Adding explicit negative constraints ('no explanations, no other text') directly addresses the behavioral source, and declaring the automated pipeline context shifts Claude's implicit audience model from human to machine, which is a strong signal to suppress elaboration. This is the canonical fix for structured-output compliance: constrain the format in the prompt, not in post-processing.

distractors.A: A regex extractor is technically viable but architecturally fragile — it converts a deterministic parsing contract into a heuristic extraction problem. The failure mode is when Claude produces a response like 'I'd classify this as neither safe nor violating' where the taxonomy term is absent or paraphrased, the regex silently picks the wrong label or returns null. You've also now maintained two systems instead of fixing one. A is the right answer only when you can't touch the prompt — here you can.
distractors.C: Temperature controls sampling stochasticity, not instruction-following fidelity. Verbosity in this case isn't caused by high-variance token sampling — it's caused by the model correctly inferring that a human-facing classifier should explain its reasoning. You can run temperature 0.0 and still get 'This post appears borderline' on every call. The misconception is conflating output length with sampling entropy; in production this change burns latency budget and delivers no structural fix.
distractors.D: Instruction-following models are not architecturally unsuited to single-token outputs — that's the error. Claude handles enumerated classification tasks well with proper prompt constraints. Fine-tuning introduces a full MLOps surface: training data curation, label drift, retraining cycles, and a separate deployment. That cost is only justified when prompt engineering hits a ceiling, which hasn't been reached here.

mental_model: Whenever a downstream parser has strict format expectations, the fix lives at the prompt layer first — explicit output schema, negative constraints, and pipeline context declaration. Reach for post-processing or model replacement only after prompt-level constraints are exhausted, because those approaches shift brittleness downstream rather than eliminating it.
cross_domain_note: This pattern maps directly to the trust boundary domain: declaring 'this output is consumed by an automated pipeline' is functionally equivalent to switching the trust context, which Claude uses to calibrate response style. The same mechanism applies when designing agentic tool-call responses where verbose assistant turns break JSON parsing in the orchestration layer.

why_correct: The architectural principle is that role declaration and behavioral constraints are distinct prompt components — a classifier role tells Claude *what to do*, but negative constraints tell it *what not to do*, and these must be explicit. Relying on implicit inference from a role persona to suppress verbose output is an underdefined instruction set; the model has no hard constraint against being helpful with explanations, so in ambiguous or edge-case inputs it will drift toward its RLHF-trained default of explanatory, conversational output. For a zero-tolerance pipeline, you need explicit prohibitions ('return only the label', 'output is consumed by automated systems, not humans') because the automated-pipeline audience signal actively suppresses the helpfulness heuristic that generates explanatory text.

distractors.A: The failure mode here is treating 'persistent' as equivalent to 'sufficient' — the system prompt does persist, but persistence only means the instruction fires every turn, not that the instruction set is complete. A sparse system prompt that declares role without explicit negative constraints leaves verbose output behavior underspecified. Per-message constraint repetition is a different (and often redundant) pattern, but the actual fix is writing a complete system prompt the first time, not repeating it in user turns.
distractors.C: This is the structured-output-as-silver-bullet misconception. Structured output API parameters constrain the *schema* of the response, not the *verbosity within fields* — a model can still return a JSON object with a 'label' field plus an unprompted 'reasoning' field if the schema permits strings. Prompt-based and API-level controls are complementary layers, not competing ones; mixing them is correct defense-in-depth, not a conflict.
distractors.D: Prompt length doesn't drive taxonomy hallucination — grounding does. A well-enumerated taxonomy in the system prompt is exactly what anchors the output space. This inverts the real risk: an underspecified system prompt with no explicit label enumeration is what produces out-of-distribution labels.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic for classifier prompt design: role sets the task frame, explicit enumeration grounds the output space, and negative constraints close the verbose-output escape hatch — all three must be present in the system prompt. Any behavioral property you need to hold at production SLA must be stated, not inferred.
cross_domain_note: This connects directly to agentic pipeline trust boundary design — when Claude is a non-interactive node in an orchestration layer, the prompt must encode the consumer context explicitly, because the model's default behavior is calibrated for human-facing helpfulness, not machine-to-machine terseness.

why_correct: Progressive summarization solves the root cause — information density collapse at the front of the context window — by converting verbose turn-by-turn history into a compact, semantically dense summary that preserves the complaint lineage and resolution state without consuming proportional tokens. The architectural principle is: token budget should scale with information value, not message count. Recent turns get full fidelity because they carry actionable state; older turns get compressed because their marginal token cost exceeds their marginal retrieval value. This pattern holds per-session cost near-constant regardless of session length, which is exactly the cost constraint the product team imposed.

distractors.A: Upgrading context window size is a ceiling raise, not a fix — it delays the failure without eliminating it. Any sufficiently long billing dispute session will still exhaust even a 200K token window if naive append-only history management continues. Cost also scales directly with input tokens, so a larger window model increases per-session spend on every call, not just edge cases. This answer is tempting because it's operationally simple, but it violates the cost constraint and defers rather than resolves the root cause.
distractors.C: Increasing the truncation threshold from 15 to 30 turns is the same architectural error as A — it shifts the failure point without addressing the mechanism. The bot will still ask users to re-explain their complaint; it just happens at turn 50 instead of turn 40. The QA gap (15 turns vs. 40+ turns in production) should signal that any fixed-turn truncation strategy will eventually fail as session length distribution drifts. This is the 'raise the waterline' anti-pattern — it looks like a fix until production load pushes past the new threshold.
distractors.D: RAG over conversation history introduces retrieval non-determinism into what should be a coherent conversational state machine. Top-k semantic similarity doesn't guarantee chronological coherence or causal chain preservation — the model may retrieve the user's original complaint but miss the three clarifications that followed. In a billing dispute context, missing an intermediate concession or agreed partial credit is a correctness failure, not just a quality dip. Retrieval latency also adds per-turn overhead that compounds across a 40-turn session.

mental_model: When the failure mode is 'model loses early-session state under long conversations,' the decision tree is: Does the information have uniform value over time? If no — and in support workflows it never does — use compression, not extension. Progressive summarization is the canonical pattern because it trades token volume for semantic density while preserving causal continuity, which pure truncation and pure retrieval both fail to do.
cross_domain_note: This pattern intersects directly with agentic loop design: any multi-step agent that accumulates tool call results faces the same token budget vs. state fidelity tradeoff, and progressive summarization of intermediate scratchpad state is the same architectural solution applied to tool output rather than conversation turns.

why_correct: Answer A implements the compression-before-eviction pattern: before dropping turns from the context window, you distill their semantic content into a persistent summary that rides along in every subsequent call. The summary becomes a synthetic grounding artifact — the model sees the account number as established fact without needing raw turn 3 in the window. This preserves conversational continuity through the entire session while keeping each API call within token budget, which is exactly what naive slicing destroys.

distractors.B: The failure mode here is that B solves the overflow problem without solving the amnesia problem. Token-aware truncation is strictly better than turn-count truncation for budget management, and you'd want it regardless — but it's orthogonal to continuity loss. Once turn 3 eventually ages out under any sliding-window scheme, the bot still forgets the account number. B is the right answer to 'how do I truncate more precisely?' not 'how do I preserve critical context?'
distractors.C: This is the persistence-without-grounding anti-pattern. Storing full history in a database is fine for audit trails and session replay, but passing only the last 20 turns to the API means the model's context window still has the same hole. 'Rely on the model to infer missing context' is not a retrieval strategy — it's asking the model to hallucinate plausibly. In production this produces confident wrong answers, exactly the account-number re-prompt you're already seeing.
distractors.D: max_tokens controls output generation length, not input context capacity. The overflow error is on the input side — the request payload exceeds the model's context window. Increasing max_tokens makes the problem worse by consuming more of the output budget, not more of the input capacity.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic for sliding-window context management: slicing without summarizing trades context overflow for semantic amnesia. Any time you evict turns, you must decide whether their information is truly disposable or load-bearing — if load-bearing, compress and carry forward before eviction. Summarize-then-slice is the only pattern that satisfies both the token budget constraint and the continuity invariant.
cross_domain_note: This summarization-as-grounding pattern is structurally identical to what you'd implement in a RAG pipeline — retrieved chunks substitute for raw source documents in the context window. The same 'distilled facts injected at a privileged position' mechanic applies whether you're compressing conversation history or chunking a knowledge base.

why_correct: The API key is a bearer credential — whoever holds it has full account access, no identity verification, no per-user scoping. Bundled client-side JavaScript is fully readable by any user via DevTools, source maps, or network inspection, so the key is effectively public the moment the app ships. The trust boundary in this architecture must sit at the server; the server authenticates to Anthropic, and you separately authenticate your users to your server. This is the same principle behind why OAuth client secrets never live in SPAs.

distractors.A: The Anthropic API is a standard HTTPS endpoint — any HTTP client including `fetch()` can set arbitrary JSON body fields including `system`. There's no browser-specific parameter restriction. This would be relevant if the API used a browser-restricted transport mechanism, which it doesn't.
distractors.C: This inverts the architecture: server-side proxies can implement server-side caching (Redis, in-memory, semantic cache via embeddings), but that has nothing to do with browser localStorage. Conflating server cache with client storage is the failure mode — and storing API responses in localStorage introduces its own data leakage surface for sensitive support ticket content.
distractors.D: CORS is configured by the server receiving the request. Anthropic could allow cross-origin requests if they chose to; the CORS policy isn't unconditional or inherent to the protocol. The real problem is credential exposure, not CORS headers — this distractor mistakes a configurable policy for a hard architectural constraint.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic: anything that authenticates to a third-party service on behalf of your account belongs behind your trust boundary. If a credential can be extracted by a user with browser DevTools, it's already compromised. Server-side proxy isn't about latency or caching — it's about keeping the authentication surface inside the perimeter you control.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to prompt injection defense: both problems share the same root — you cannot trust input or credentials that cross the client-side trust boundary. Architectures that expose API keys client-side are also typically vulnerable to users manipulating the system prompt by intercepting and replaying modified requests.

why_correct: The root cause is non-deterministic output formatting driven by input complexity — Claude's default behavior calibrates response length and structure to the apparent complexity of the input, not to the constraints of your rendering surface. The system prompt is the correct trust boundary for invariant, application-wide constraints because it applies uniformly to every request regardless of what's in the human turn. Explicit format constraints — word ceiling, plain-text only, required sections, audience framing — shift format control from Claude's heuristic judgment to your architectural specification. The post-processing layer then becomes a safety net rather than the primary control plane, which is the only defensible production pattern.

distractors.A: Moving format instructions to the user turn is the right call when you need per-request format variation — e.g., different output schemas for different downstream consumers. The constraint here is that the dashboard is fixed and uniform; every response needs the same format contract. User-turn instructions also introduce a prompt injection surface if ticket content is user-supplied and bleeds into the same turn as your format directives.
distractors.C: Temperature governs token-level sampling distribution, not structural output length or markdown usage. Lowering temperature makes outputs more deterministic in word choice and less prone to creative drift, but it has no meaningful effect on whether Claude decides to use headers or write 600 words. The failure mode is that you tune temperature, observe slightly less variance in a few test runs, ship it, and the same overflow failures reappear at scale.
distractors.D: Sentence-boundary truncation is a local fix to the symptom — incoherent cuts — not the root cause, which is that outputs are oversized and unstructured in the first place. You're still running a 600-word plain-text extraction pipeline when the spec calls for a 200-word briefing. Coherent truncation of an oversized response is not the same as a correctly scoped response.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic for rendering-surface constraints: anything the downstream system cannot negotiate — fixed container size, no markdown, required schema — belongs in the system prompt as an invariant, not in post-processing. Post-processing should validate and sanitize, not compensate for absent upstream constraints. If your normalization layer is doing load-bearing work, you have a missing system prompt specification.
cross_domain_note: This pattern maps directly to cost and latency management: oversized outputs burn token budget on every request. Explicit output constraints in the system prompt are one of the cheapest throughput optimizations available — you get format correctness and reduced output token spend simultaneously.
